Kastner consulted on a utility running remote meter reading over power lines at five baud.
Technology used by a utility client of Arthur D. Little Systems; data transmitted at five baud over power lines.
Kastner operated IBM 360/65 at MIT and Cornell; ran SysGen at Cornell.
Kastner self-taught FORTRAN at MIT as computer operator.
Kastner self-taught assembler at MIT alongside FORTRAN.
Kastner encountered ELIZA during his MIT computer operator period; early AI exposure.
Code generator developed during Kastner's time at PHI; notable early automation tool.
Stratus Pair-and-Spare architecture; IBM OEM System 88; competitive with Tandem NonStop.
OEM fault-tolerant system produced by Stratus and sold under IBM branding.
Described by DEC as 'most important launch after VAX.' Kastner led the launch.
Target of secret DEC blind benchmark; IBM responded with DB2 v2.
TPC-A (1989), TPC-B (1990), TPC-C (1992), TPC-H, TPC-DS, TPC-E. Kastner was co-founder and early auditor.
Emerging during Prime Computer peak period (1979-1981); key technology transition witnessed by Kastner.
Dominant computing paradigm during Aberdeen's partnership years 1990-1997.
RDBMS dominance was a defining technology trend of Aberdeen's partnership years.
Kastner predicted microprocessor OLTP dominance in 1987 Yankee Group paper; TPC benchmarks standardized OLTP measurement.
Aberdeen proprietary research methodology developed by Kastner and partners.
Aberdeen proprietary benchmark research methodology developed alongside SNR.
Key technology transition Kastner witnessed during expert witness / Intel advisory period.
SaaS emergence was a defining transition of Kastner's Aberdeen final years (2004-2008).
AI/ML maturation and generative AI represent the final major technology transition in the timeline (2015-2026).
Platform used at PHI service bureau for client work including Cumberland Farms and First Chicago DDA.
Internet boom and e-commerce infrastructure was the defining transition of Aberdeen's scaled years 1997-2001.

Present at both MIT (dedicated development machine with graphics workstation and trackball) and Cornell (main production mainframe). FORTRAN airflow-analysis code written on this machine was still embedded in climate models as late as the 2020s.
Used at MIT as a reader-printer workhorse: ingested punch-card decks, wrote to magnetic tape at 800 bpi, and printed output from the mainframe. Described as 'the loading dock.'
MIT production mainframe in 1966-1967. Ran timesharing during daytime hours for dozens of academic users; batch mode off-peak. Timesharing techniques developed here fed directly into Multics.
Cornell's Sysgen target was a modified version of IBM OS/360, enhanced with HASP. IBM provided OS source code to customers at this time—a practice abandoned in the 1980s.

Electromechanical sorters, collators, and accumulators programmed via plugboard wiring. Used at Cornell's Hotel Ezra Cornell weekend. Could not perform conditional logic flexibly enough to automate hotel accounting.
Kastner taught himself FORTRAN at MIT on overnight shifts. Used for CHASE simulation at Cornell; floating-point arithmetic caused accounting discrepancies that Kastner fixed.
Kastner taught himself IBM assembler on the company's idle overnight CPU cycles at MIT in 1966-1967.

Designed to merge FORTRAN's mathematical capability with COBOL's data handling. Cornell sought experienced PL/1 programmers in 1967 — virtually none existed. Kastner compared the ask to seeking experienced ChatGPT programmers in 2020.
Referenced as the business data-processing language whose structured data and file-management conventions PL/1 attempted to absorb alongside FORTRAN's scientific capabilities.
Produced by Project MAC at MIT; its foundations came from timesharing work on the IBM 7094. Referenced as resurfacing in Kastner's career at Stratus Computer.
Used at MIT's Project MAC area to manipulate symbolic representations of knowledge for AI research. Kastner observed LISP in use circa 1966-1967.
Text-based conversational program at MIT that 'unnerved people who didn't know it had no inner life.' Kastner observed it circa 1966-1967 and concluded AI was just code competing for machine time.
Appeared permanently installed on the IBM 360/65 graphics workstation at MIT. Kastner could play it on a '$5 million mainframe' during overnight shifts circa 1966-1967.
Early PDP/8 minicomputers were appearing in Cambridge labs in 1966-1967. Kastner identified them as the beginning of the end of the centralized glass-house computing era.
Added to Cornell's OS/360 to manage the queue of jobs waiting to run. Used during the September 23, 1967 Sysgen with Arlene Larsen.

The dominant computing model of the 1960s: submit a job and wait. Defined the economics of computing for a decade. The MIT three-machine pipeline (cards-in, tape to mainframe, results out) was Kastner's hands-on introduction.
Dozens of academic users typed at alphanumeric terminals simultaneously on the MIT IBM 7094. Kastner characterized it as 'brilliant but fragile.'
IBM 1401 at MIT wrote punch-card batches to magnetic tape at 800 bits per inch for transfer to the mainframe.
13 megabytes of storage, described as 'an enormous amount of storage by the standards of the day.' Used during Cornell's Sysgen on September 23, 1967.

tech_name: Remote Job Entry (56 kbps telephone line)
notes: Cornell's innovative workaround after IBM couldn't deliver the 360/67: RJE over a 56-kilobit dedicated telephone line from Upson Hall to Langmuir Labs. Kastner called this effectively Cornell's 'cloud' — a concept without a name for another forty years.
Installed at Upson Hall, Cornell; read student card decks and transmitted them to the IBM 360/65 at Langmuir Labs via the 56 kbps telephone line.
Kastner's primary tool in the Cape Cod restaurant bookkeeping department as a child. Cornell's Statistics 101 lab previously required hand-cranked mechanical calculators before Kastner automated it.
Timesharing-capable variant that Cornell originally planned to install to match MIT's capability. IBM could not deliver it, forcing Cornell's RJE workaround.

CHASE = Cornell Hotel School Administration Simulation Exercise; CRASE = Cornell Restaurant Administration Simulation Exercise; CHESS = Cornell Educational Strategic Simulation. Kastner enhanced and debugged CHASE in 1967-68, fixing FORTRAN floating-point arithmetic errors. Adopted by Intercontinental, Marriott, Howard Johnson's, and KFC.
Main computing platform at PHI service bureau; CPU time cost $360/hour in 1969.
Family platform for PHI service bureau operations; reference card was primary documentation resource.
Primary language used at PHI; Siegel's One-Instruction Switch optimization was demonstrable in COBOL; Cosmos generated millions of lines of architecturally sound COBOL.

Code-generation system at PHI that produced millions of lines of COBOL per specification. Originally called Customer Accounting System (CAS). Named after Creedence Clearwater Revival album 'Cosmos Factory'. Enabled a team of four to build an entire bank checking account system in one year.
Operating system later architected by Robert A. Siegel after he left PHI.
Two-digit year field vulnerability; Kastner fixed an early instance in Marine Midland Bank's bond portfolio system in July 1969, 30 years before the global Y2K crisis.
Command syntax for IBM OS to control program execution: tape mounts, disk pack allocation, memory reservation. Notoriously cryptic; a single misplaced comma could fail a job.
Primary input mechanism at PHI; programmers wrote code on coding forms, had it keypunched, submitted deck to operations queue, waited hours for printout.
Primary memory technology of the IBM 360 era; core dumps captured entire contents of core memory upon program failure.
Debugging technique: on catastrophic failure, OS printed entire core memory contents as hex. Could span hundreds of pages. Kastner learned to read these to find program errors.
Dense fold-out cheat sheet roughly placemat-sized; primary documentation resource for programmers at PHI in absence of online docs.
Business model where companies purchased CPU time on shared mainframes; PHI charged $360/hour for IBM 360/65 time in 1969.
Interactive terminal-based computing available at universities and research labs in 1969 but not typical at commercial service bureaus like PHI.
Banking application upgraded by Kastner in July 1969; contained two-digit year field vulnerability for bonds maturing in year 2000+.
Accounting system designed by Kastner for Cumberland Farms; required handling mechanical pump counter rollover at 9,999 gallons.
Technique exemplified by Cosmos: templates and rules produce architecturally consistent code at scale, sidestepping Brooks's Law coordination overhead.
Navigation code for the Apollo lunar program; PHI founders Philip Hankins and David Moros had written this code before founding PHI.
Chase Manhattan migration produced 25 million lines of bug-free COBOL code in one year.
96-column format used by Chase Manhattan; incompatible with IBM 80-column cards; required field explosion during migration.
Alias for Univac 96-column punch card; 16 extra columns vs. IBM 80-column caused field boundary explosion in Chase migration.
Source platform for United Shoe Machinery migration; violated architectural physics when ported to Data General Eclipse.
Target platform for United Shoe Machinery migration; architectural mismatch with IBM 4300 caused thrashing at CPU, application, disk, and management layers.
Proposed for Wisconsin Electric remote meter reading; signals superimposed on 60-Hz power current at 5 baud; no new wiring required.
Wisconsin Electric project; rural service territory with party telephone lines; 5-baud power-line solution selected over costly dedicated data lines.
Rio customs inspector unspooled tape reel searching for forbidden images; recorded at 800 bits per inch.
Platform at Houghton Mifflin; incompatible with Kastner’s IBM-era tools and habits.
Prime’s minicomputer platform family; sold as solutions (banking, engineering) rather than raw MIPS; ran PRIMOS operating system and INFORMATION database environment.
Specific Prime minicomputer model; the Prime Office system delivered to the White House ran on a Model 750; also used as base for Prime INFORMATION deployments.
Prime’s proprietary operating system; bundled with Prime hardware and INFORMATION database; customers discovered they had bought into the PRIMOS ecosystem after purchasing a business solution.

Database and application development environment based on the Pick operating system; used multi-value dynamic arrays instead of relational tables; strong for commercial apps but created proprietary lock-in; sold as 4GL-like environment.

Architecture underlying Prime INFORMATION; developed in the 1960s for inventory/distribution; organized data into multi-value dynamic array fields; strong for commercial apps but non-standard and non-relational.
Prime INFORMATION’s application development environment described as a 4GL database environment; positioned as faster than COBOL-based development.

Prime Office was the company’s thrust into office automation; Schlegel led the launch; a system running on a Prime 750 was installed at the White House as the first office automation system at federal executive level.

Case-management system developed by INSLAW under a federal LEAA grant; tracked defendants, charges, hearings, evidence; Prime won hardware contracts for 20+ systems; later subject of DOJ theft allegations and congressional investigations.

Identified by Kastner as the architectural force that eventually made Prime INFORMATION’s non-relational, proprietary model a strategic liability; lock-in became a prison when relational databases grew through the mid-1980s.
Prime INFORMATION positioned as eliminating the need for ‘an army of COBOL programmers’; contrasted with INFORMATION’s faster application development model.
Prime’s primary competitive reference; Prime positioned its INFORMATION/PRIMOS stack against the VAX ecosystem on faster application cycles and tighter integration.
Kastner identifies standardized x86 architecture combined with open operating systems as the economic force that ultimately overwhelmed all proprietary minicomputer platforms including Prime.

Open operating system that, combined with x86 economics, rendered Prime’s minicomputer architecture economically irrelevant by the late 1980s; specifically cited by Kastner: ‘Intel-based server running Unix’.
General category for Prime’s 32-bit minicomputer family; competed against DEC, Data General, and HP in analyst comparisons.
CAD/CAM applications at Prime customers like Ford classified as technical/scientific, removing them from Kastner’s commercial territory.
General category: computing architectures designed to prevent any interruption from hardware failures.
Four microprocessors arranged in two pairs with hardware comparator; failure handled without software awareness or lost clock cycles.
Software-based fault tolerance using processor pairs and application-level checkpointing; required specialized programmer effort.
First Stratus product; announced December 1981; rebranded by IBM as the IBM System/88 under OEM agreement.
IBM-branded version of Stratus FT200; OEM deal enabled Stratus marketing message 'The Technology IBM Chose.'
OEM deal in which IBM rebranded Stratus FT200 as IBM System/88; strategically neutralized startup-risk objections.
Built on Motorola 68020 processors; Kastner led market and product team for this announcement; preserved lockstep architecture.
Microprocessor used in Stratus XA 2000; enabled dramatic performance gains vs. previous Stratus hardware.
Standard programming language on Stratus/System/88; Kastner's 'zero-code advantage' message: standard COBOL worked without fault-tolerance modifications.
Also cited alongside COBOL as standard language usable without modification on Stratus fault-tolerant hardware.
Central market use case for fault-tolerant hardware; Kastner's 1987 Yankee Group report predicted OLTP price-performance explosion.
Telecom signaling protocol; Stratus became embedded infrastructure for SS7 signaling systems after telecom market breakthrough.
Approach contrasted with software fault tolerance (Tandem); hides failures from application software entirely.
Referenced as example of non-fault-tolerant mainframe that crashed on component failure; central to Kastner's market problem statement.
Kastner's downtime-cost calculator framework: quantify cost of downtime per hour vs. hardware price premium to justify fault-tolerant investment.
100-page report published January 1987; predicted microprocessor-based OLTP price-performance explosion and mainframe displacement via Moore's Law.
Kastner invoked Moore's Law as driver for microprocessor OLTP economics overtaking mainframes in the 1987 Yankee Group report.
Central DEC platform; the multiprocessor VAX 8200 was the System Under Test in DECtp benchmarks; Kastner calls it 'one of the great minicomputer designs ever produced.'
32-bit CISC architecture; beating IBM mainframe on cost-performance only to itself be displaced by x86 within four years.
DEC's operating system for VAX; provided the software environment for DECtp OLTP workloads.
DEC's relational database; described as 'a genuine relational database' and 'transaction-friendly'; combined with OLTP monitor for DECtp; later acquired by Oracle.
Integrated OLTP platform comprising multiprocessor VAX, Rdb, and an acquired transaction monitor; launched 1988 with a ~$2 million marketing budget.
Core workload type for banking, insurance, retail, financial services; IBM mainframe franchise being challenged by DEC VAX in 1987-1988.
IBM's flagship mainframe; 3090-400 (4-processor) used as System Under Test and 3090-200 as load driver in the Westwood benchmark; exposed SNA Tax.
Communications framework connecting 3270 terminals to mainframe applications; source of the 'SNA Tax' — massive per-session initialization overhead via VTAM.
IBM's flagship relational database; benchmarked as having been tuned primarily for query workloads rather than transactional throughput; IBM announced DB2 Version 2 in October 1988 in response.
Standard IBM mainframe terminal; each session required 1+ CPU-second of VTAM initialization overhead — the core of the SNA Tax calculation.
CICS application consulting firm in Westwood, MA used as cover to conduct the blind benchmark against IBM's 3090.
IBM network architecture; generated the 'SNA Tax' discovered during Westwood benchmark — over an hour of clock time just to establish 10,000 terminal sessions.
Standard OLTP benchmark simulating bank account transaction processing; measures TPS with 100 virtual terminals per claimed TPS; called the 'four-minute mile' of enterprise computing.
First formal TPC benchmark; Kastner audited Stratus TPC-A submission; based on the debit-credit benchmark but with full pricing, independent audit, and public disclosure requirements.
Second TPC benchmark; batch variant of TPC-A; part of the suite that replaced vendor-proprietary benchmark claims.
Complex multi-table order-entry workload benchmark; became the definitive OLTP server benchmark through the 1990s and 2000s; results published quarterly in trade press.
TPC decision-support benchmark; later addition to TPC suite covering analytical/query workloads.
Proprietary benchmark jointly used by Stratus and IBM; Kastner audited the Stratus/IBM RAMP-C submission as part of Aberdeen Transaction Services.
Groupe Bull mainframe-class system; Kastner audited three models in US, Italy, and France for TPC compliance.
Multiprocessor Intel 386 systems predicted in Kastner's 1987 Yankee Group report as arriving on the horizon; by 1990 x86 servers (Compaq et al.) undercut DEC VAX OLTP pricing.
Cited by Kastner as the economic driver meeting departmental transaction processing economics — making Intel 386-based servers viable for OLTP by early 1990s.
IBM's mainframe communications manager for SNA; required 1+ CPU-second to initialize each 3270 terminal session — the source of the SNA Tax at scale.
Multiprocessor VAX system used as the DEC system in the DECtp vs. IBM 3090 benchmark; delivered comparable OLTP throughput at a fraction of 3090 cost.
Foundational VAX model; referenced in chapter context as the baseline that established the VAX architecture franchise Kastner was defending.
Aberdeen championed client-server as a permanent architectural principle separating presentation, business logic, and data management. Kastner argued this before the term microservices was coined.
Kastner’s personal mission 1985–1995: championing RDBMS that could handle both OLTP and query/reporting. Aberdeen predicted relational model would dominate universally.

vendor: multiple (AT&T, Sun, HP, DEC, IBM, SCO)
notes: Aberdeen correctly predicted open-systems transition and Unix’s role; missed that fragmentation would persist rather than standardize.
Aberdeen hired a dedicated Linux analyst when Red Hat took off. Linux won open-architecture battle through open licensing and community development rather than technical standardization.
Aberdeen research example: 'Should you deploy Windows NT or Unix for database servers?' A core client decision topic in the 1990s.
Aberdeen built a data warehousing practice before most enterprises knew what a data warehouse was. Kastner claims a circa-1991 slide of today’s AI-driven analysis model.
Logan had published Kastner’s Yankee Group study on transaction processing. Aberdeen championed RDBMS capable of OLTP plus query/reporting.
Aberdeen predicted integration complexity would accelerate as client-server proliferated, driving investment in EAI middleware platforms.
Downstream outcome of Aberdeen’s integration-complexity thesis; Aberdeen’s early integration research anticipated SOA and API management investment.

Aberdeen’s proprietary framework for integrating heterogeneous systems. Systems-of-record at center; middle manager rings run productivity apps; productivity nodes access via LAN. Described as ‘Lego for building systems architectures’ and a business planning tool for integration problem acceleration.
Kastner’s coverage domain at Aberdeen included x86 servers. The cost advantages of standardized x86 components underpinned Aberdeen’s open-architecture thesis.
Aberdeen’s open-systems transition call included the RISC-to-x86 shift as part of the proprietary-to-commodity trajectory.
One of the three RDBMS products compared in Aberdeen OLTP performance research (with Sybase and Informix).
One of the three RDBMS products compared in Aberdeen OLTP performance research. Aberdeen published comparative analysis of Oracle, Sybase, and Informix.
One of three RDBMS products in Aberdeen’s OLTP performance comparisons. Later acquired by IBM.
Aberdeen touted thin-client hardware; Kastner acknowledges this as a miss because the market chose client-server where the browser became ever-heavier and complex.
Kastner notes Aberdeen’s client-server architectural principle anticipated microservices before that term was coined.
Aberdeen predicted hierarchical and network databases would remain niche as relational model won universally. This proved correct.
Aberdeen championed RDBMS and SQL flexibility combined with ACID transaction guarantees as the universal standard. Kastner notes C++ ORM persisted rather than object databases supplanting SQL.

Aberdeen built a dedicated data warehousing practice in the early 1990s; correctly predicted every significant enterprise would eventually build one. Technologies evolved from Teradata to columnar stores to cloud warehouses.

Kastner argued by 2000 that SOA was the decade's defining architectural shift, comparable to client-server in the 1990s. Standards (SOAP, WSDL, UDDI) were immature. REST APIs eventually displaced heavyweight SOAP-based SOA. Kastner completed nine SOA studies in nine months before departing Aberdeen in mid-2007.
Part of the SOA standards triplet (SOAP, WSDL, UDDI) that Kastner noted were immature during early SOA adoption; eventually displaced by REST for most use cases.
Part of the SOA standards triplet; cited as immature during early SOA adoption window.
Part of the SOA standards triplet; cited as immature; largely abandoned in practice as REST architectures dominated.
Kastner notes that by 2008-2010, REST APIs and lightweight service approaches displaced heavyweight SOAP-based SOA while preserving core architectural principles.
Named as an example of open-source internet infrastructure (alongside Apache, MySQL) that Kastner predicted would move into enterprise applications and middleware.

Kastner predicted early 2000s that open source would move from internet infrastructure into enterprise applications and middleware; adoption played out exactly as predicted across 2000-2006, though taking the full decade rather than projected 2-3 years.
Named alongside Linux and Apache as open-source internet infrastructure; also cited as penetrating enterprise infrastructure by 2000-2006.
Named as one of the open-source databases that penetrated enterprise infrastructure during 2000-2006, confirming Kastner's prediction.
Kastner mentions full LAMP stacks (Linux, Apache, MySQL, PHP/Perl/Python) penetrating enterprise infrastructure as confirmation of his open-source enterprise adoption prediction.
EAI platforms named as part of the integration technology market explosion across 2000-2006, alongside ETL tools, data quality software, MDM systems, and SOA infrastructure.
CRM named as a new coverage domain Aberdeen expanded into at scale.
Kastner references 'BlackBerry keyboards' in 2005 as the state of smartphones before the iPhone touchscreen, in context of consumerization-of-IT prediction.
Kastner alludes to the coming iPhone touchscreen era (2007+) as the confirmation of his consumerization-of-IT prediction made by 2004-2005.

Aberdeen's TCO calculators incorporated hardware, software licensing, implementation services, maintenance, training, and operational overhead over 3-5 year horizons; became Aberdeen's most commercially potent tool; challenged conventional sticker-price procurement logic.

Systematic survey approaches reaching hundreds of enterprises on technology adoption patterns, deployment challenges, and satisfaction metrics; became a signature Aberdeen product used by CIOs to justify budget decisions and by vendors in sales materials.

Systematic capture and reuse of successful enterprise deployment decisions (architectural, organizational, vendor, PM) as reusable frameworks; described as knowledge management before the term became fashionable.
Named as part of the integration technology market explosion across 2000-2006 confirming Kastner's 'data integration as perpetual tax' thesis.
Named alongside EAI and ETL as part of the data integration market expansion, confirming N-squared integration complexity thesis.
Enterprise investment in e-commerce infrastructure during internet bubble period; described as driven by optimistic ROI assumptions that couldn't survive scrutiny.
Named as a key investment driver during the internet bubble; every internet startup and enterprise digital initiative needed analyst validation.
Central technology in Safeway litigation; described as strategic infrastructure for large retailers by mid-1990s.
Teradata’s data warehouse machine physically destroyed when fire suppression system exploded in Safeway’s computer room.
First expert witness engagement required expertise in relational databases at architectural and market level.
Three-tier IBM mainframe system proposed for Florida welfare RFP; Kastner testified IBM’s own estimator showed 20–30 second response times.
Referenced in software project failure pattern section; ERP implementations frequently encountered Y2K remediation priority conflicts.
Kastner notes agile modestly improved IT project success rates; Standish CHAOS Report findings confirmed partial improvement.
Mentioned as reducing but not eliminating fundamental software project failure rates per CHAOS Report.
Aberdeen TCO models applied directly in Safeway and other litigation to quantify the market value of contracted vs. delivered technology.
Aberdeen and TPC benchmarking frameworks used to evaluate technology performance in litigation; Kastner’s TPC credentials recognized by courts.
Kastner’s TPC work gave him credentials courts found credible for technology expert testimony.
Referenced in context of data warehouse value to large retailers like Safeway: product placement, promotional pricing, inventory management.
Kastner taught himself FORTRAN at MIT's computer room in 1966 on a $5 million mainframe at $360/hour.
The $5M mainframe at MIT costing $360/hour defined the early scarcity economics of computing.
Economically (not technically) displaced by minicomputers for departmental workloads; Pattern 1 exemplar.
Democratized compute access by 1972; economically displaced by x86 servers; proprietary margins funded x86 development that displaced them.
By time Kastner left Stratus in 1987, x86 was preparing to displace all proprietary minicomputer and workstation platforms.
Not technically inferior to Linux; lost on licensing economics and community development (Pattern 1). IBM cited as funding Unix research (Pattern 2).
Won over Unix on licensing economics and community development. Kastner notes he got the destination right (Unix dominance to open source) but wrong mechanism.
Kastner claims credit for predicting RDBMS dominance for operational systems; listed in the 'got right' column.
One of the architectural responses to the integration tax Kastner named in the early 1990s; did not eliminate integration complexity.
Kastner cites SOA as directionally correct prediction, five years early (adoption timeline bias).
Part of integration architecture evolution; each new layer solved integration problem while enabling new proliferation generating new complexity.

A significant analytical miss for Kastner: he understood compute commoditization but did not anticipate infrastructure migrating to shared utility services rather than enterprise-owned commodity hardware.
First chatbot (1966); demonstrated that pattern-matching rules could simulate conversation; raised Turing Test implications. No actual language understanding.
Rule-based AI with explicit knowledge encoded by human experts. MYCIN and XCON named as prominent examples. Limited by knowledge acquisition bottleneck and brittleness outside narrow design domains.
Medical diagnosis expert system; cited in AI sidebar as representative of 1980s rule-based AI.
Computer configuration expert system for DEC; cited in AI sidebar as representative commercial expert system of the 1980s.
Named as part of early AI research energy at MIT in 1966, alongside ELIZA and Project MAC.
MIT research project active in 1966 when Kastner was there; cited as part of the early AI research environment.
1990s generation: decision trees, neural networks, support vector machines. Required labeled training data; worked on specific well-defined tasks. Transition from rules to statistics.
Multi-layer neural networks enabled by GPU computing and large datasets. AlexNet (2012) cited as demonstration that deep learning outperformed human-engineered features on image classification.
2012 deep learning breakthrough for image classification; cited in AI sidebar as milestone for deep learning's superiority over human-engineered features.
"Attention is All You Need" (2017); self-attention mechanisms enabling parallel sequence processing and training on vastly larger datasets. Foundation for current LLMs.

GPT series, Claude, Gemini. Trained on internet-scale text. Emergent capabilities at scale. First AI systems that handle ambiguity, context, and implicit knowledge qualitatively differently than predecessors.
OpenAI's large language model series; referenced as exemplar of current generative AI generation.
Kastner's 2026 assessment: doing to knowledge work what the minicomputer did to batch processing in the 1970s. Impact on workers positive and negative. Adoption primarily a 2030s story.
Part of the automation journey from batch processing to the web; referenced in the "question the 12-year-old was asking" narrative.
Part of the automation progression from batch processing; referenced in context of Federal Reserve check-clearing automation.
Kastner's biggest admitted miss: underestimated how thoroughly the smartphone would transform enterprise IT. Mobile-first architecture implications larger than recognized in 2005.
Modern POS running on a tablet can automate the bookkeeping work that required three people in the summer season; used as closing illustration.
Kastner got direction right, two years early on adoption; missed how quickly it became full application platform rather than just information medium.
First stage in the automation progression described in the closing section; preceded batch processing.
CPU time cost $360/hour in 1969; used to illustrate the scarcity inversion from compute to human judgment.
Kastner argues mainframe fell not because minicomputer engineering was better but because economics inverted and glass-house culture could not adapt.
Aberdeen research identified persistence of client-server architecture as a correct forecast.
Dominance of relational databases cited as one of Aberdeen's correct forecasts in the 1990s.
Kastner notes open systems transition was obvious to many by 1985; Unix predicted to standardize cleanly but Linux won on licensing, not elegance.
Won the Unix standardization contest on licensing rather than technical elegance, contrary to Aberdeen's prediction.
Stratus's niche defended by the visceral 'pull the board' demonstration, not patents.
Kastner identifies cloud as a genuine technical transition that will encounter the same organizational inertia as previous transitions.
Grouped with cloud and AI as genuine transitions subject to organizational friction.
The web moved faster than any analyst at Aberdeen predicted; cited as a major timing miss.

Named as a major computing transition; central to Prime, DEC, and Stratus chapters.
Named as a major computing transition; the Stratus fault-tolerant lab is a key setting.
Named as a major revolution the memoir covers; the client-server era is a central period.

tech_name: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act)
notes: Cited as governing regulation raising liability for improperly sanitized hard drives containing medical data.
EU Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment order; effective August 2005; less than 10% of UK firms were compliance-ready in summer 2003.
Only commercially viable method for eliminating data: overwrite every sector multiple times with random patterns; labor-intensive without specialized equipment.
Destruction method used for non-working drives where overwriting is not possible.
Enables >400 MHz DRAM operation; higher bandwidth, lower power than DDR1; not pin-compatible with DDR1. Platforms expected DDR2-based for 2-3 year cycle.
Point-to-point serial I/O replacing parallel PCI. x1 through x16 lane widths. PCIe x4 = 2000 MB/s aggregate; PCIe x16 graphics = ~8 GB/s vs AGP8x ~2 GB/s.
Replaces parallel ATA; thinner cables; interface opens hard-drive I/O bottleneck vs PATA.

BroadVision combines OO middleware with TP monitor technology to handle intense internet loads; key architectural differentiation.

Full enterprise management suite; $1B+ annual revenue run rate by 1997; 7,000+ customers, 200+ ISV partners, 37 OS platforms. Evolved into CA Technologies Unified Infrastructure Management. Now part of Broadcom.

Free/bundled subset of Unicenter TNG announced July 14, 1997; includes auto-discovery, object repository, 2D/3D mapping, event management, scheduling, virus scanning. 10-minute installation. 20,000 CDs distributed at CA World.
Enables ISVs and sophisticated IT orgs to build custom application management solutions using Framework as foundation.

CA's primary competitor; Kastner notes TME requires remote-network-supplied configuration estimates and 'days spent in network and asset evaluation' vs. CA's 10-minute install. Evolved into IBM Tivoli suite.
HP OpenView was dominant network management platform; Aberdeen says HP 'has clearly lost momentum' in 1997; HP's own systems division endorsed Unicenter Framework as preferred management platform.
Framework uses SNMP agents for monitoring; referenced in Unicenter TNG auto-discovery and event management.

Kastner describes 3D mapping as 'animation and elements of virtual reality to create realistic floor plans indicating location of devices within the building'; considered breakthrough enterprise management UI in 1997.
Framework initially operates on Windows NT Server; Unix versions promised for later summer 1997. NT compared to Unicenter TNG's $1B revenue run rate in Kastner's analysis.

Cabletron's market-leading network management platform; uses Inductive Modeling Technology (IMT); object-oriented distributed client/server architecture; IP for OpenSystems; major engineering investment from 1990; became Aprisma Spectrum post-Cabletron breakup; acquired by Concord then CA Technologies. Still exists as CA Spectrum as of 2020.

Policy-based VLAN and virtual networking architecture; four-phase rollout from basic switching to policy-based management; includes SecureFast SmartSwitches, VLAN Manager, Virtual Network Server; RFC 2643 documented the operational model (1999); dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.

ASIC-based switching hardware; 10M packets/second forwarding rate; up to 16,000 MAC addresses; redundant power/cooling/links; RMON support; upgrade path from shared LAN to FDDI, ATM, Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet; non-blocking switch fabric with distributed processing.

Flagship modular hub/switch chassis; integrated packet- and cell-switched backplane; 4 Gbps Internal Network Bus (INB); up to 500 ports; 10M packets/second; supports Ethernet, Token Ring, FDDI, SNA, ATM, Gigabit Ethernet; original MMAC introduced 1988 and upgradeable through all technology transitions.

Remote access product family for carrier/service provider down to SOHO markets; supports ISDN, frame relay, analog/digital modems; planned ADSL capability with Motorola Semiconductor; based on Network Express and Netlink acquisitions; Intel i960-based; dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.

Virtual LAN technology enabling logical network segmentation on shared physical infrastructure; Aberdeen notes Cabletron started VLAN work in 1990 before IEEE 802.1Q standard; IEEE 802.1Q standardized 1998; VLANs remain fundamental to modern enterprise networking.

Aberdeen's coined concept for emerging market where LAN equipment manufacturers would compete in carrier Central Office markets and vice versa; the PPSN concept partially materialized as MPLS and Metro Ethernet but was dominated by Cisco/Lucent, not Cabletron. The conceptual framework anticipated carrier-edge convergence.

Broadband access technology; Cabletron developing ADSL capability with Motorola; ADSL became mainstream broadband access technology in 2000s; now being replaced by fiber/VDSL2/cable; still deployed in some geographies.

Ipsilon's technology combining IP routing with ATM switching hardware; 5x router performance at fraction of cost; achieved via IFMP (Ipsilon Flow Management Protocol) and GSMP (Generic Switch Management Protocol); lost the standards battle to Cisco's MPLS/Tag Switching; IETF declined to standardize IFMP.

Cabletron's proprietary VLAN and policy-based network management framework; key features: connection-oriented switching, time-of-day/day-of-week access control, call-level accounting; SecureFast VLAN Operational Model published as RFC 2643 (August 1999); technology became obsolete with Cabletron's dissolution.
Protocol for managing ATM switches from IP routing layer; developed by Ipsilon; submitted to IETF; GSMP v3 eventually published as RFC 3292; technology path was superseded by MPLS.
Ipsilon's proprietary flow management protocol; core of IP Switching implementation; submitted to IETF but not adopted as standard; lost to Cisco's Tag Switching (MPLS precursor).

Cabletron's RFC 2124 protocol for policy management and accounting integration across switching platforms; one of several pre-MPLS flow management protocols that did not survive standardization process.

Cisco's competing approach to IP/ATM integration; submitted to IETF; became the basis for MPLS (Multi-Protocol Label Switching, RFC 3031); Tag Switching itself obsolete but MPLS remains fundamental to modern backbone networks.

The technology that ultimately won the IP/ATM integration standards battle; MPLS RFC 3031 published January 2001; derived from Cisco's Tag Switching; remains fundamental to carrier backbone networks, VPNs, and traffic engineering as of 2026.

Cabletron's ASIC-based switching hardware supporting VLAN, broadcast/multicast storm containment, Layer 3 virtual routing; available as modules for MMAC/MMAC-Plus chassis and standalone workgroup units; product line dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.

Aberdeen's umbrella term for front-office business applications; the category was subsequently renamed CRM (Customer Relationship Management) by Siebel, Gartner, and the analyst community; CIS as a term is now obsolete though the underlying capabilities persist in modern CRM platforms.

Subset of CIS covering lead management, opportunity tracking, account management, and sales forecasting; Aberdeen identifies SFA as the most mature CIS module at time of study; remains a core module of all modern CRM platforms.

Enabling technology linking phone systems to CRM/help desk software; Aberdeen identifies CTI as a critical CIS enabler; the technology evolved into modern UCaaS and contact-center-as-a-service (CCaaS) platforms.

Shift from client-server to internet-based CIS deployment; Aberdeen dedicates a chapter to this transition; became the dominant architecture by 2002-2005; the web paradigm eventually gave way to SaaS (Salesforce model, 1999+).

Aberdeen identifies CIS-ERP integration as a 'rapidly emerging trend'; proved highly accurate — became a major driver of SAP CRM, Oracle Siebel integration, and eventually the ERP-CRM convergence in cloud suites.
Mid-market SFA product; passed through Interact Commerce, Sage, Swiftpage; rebranded as Infor CRM then Act! Premium for Web; significantly diminished market position.
Industry-leading CRM suite; dominated market by 2000; acquired by Oracle (2005); Oracle Siebel CRM remains as on-premises legacy product; Oracle Fusion/CX is successor.
Help desk/ITSM workflow platform; Remedy ARS became BMC Remedy ITSM after 2002 acquisition; still actively used in enterprise environments as BMC Helix ITSM.

Not in study (founded 1999, post-report); included for context as the disruptive force that reshaped the entire CIS/CRM market Aberdeen was analyzing. Salesforce's SaaS model eventually displaced most vendors profiled in this report.

The de facto standard internetworking operating system; described by Aberdeen as 'unifying thread' providing cohesion to disparate networks; four cornerstones: scalability, WAN optimization, management/security, reliable adaptive routing; IOS remained dominant through 2000s; evolved into IOS XE, IOS XR for modern platforms; still the basis for Cisco's networking OS.

Cisco's per-flow packet forwarding and statistics technology; implemented in IOS 11 (1996); establishes flow cache to speed packet processing; exports per-conversation statistics; provides QoS and accounting capabilities; Aberdeen notes 10% processing overhead tradeoff; NetFlow v9 became basis for IETF IPFIX standard (RFC 7011, 2013); still widely used for network monitoring as of 2026.

Cisco's proprietary approach to combining Layer 2 switching with Layer 3 routing using simple tags; submitted to IETF; evolved into MPLS (Multi-Protocol Label Switching); RFC 3031 published 2001; Tag Switching itself replaced by MPLS standard but was the direct precursor.

The evolved and standardized version of Cisco's Tag Switching; IETF RFC 3031 (January 2001); fundamental to carrier backbone networks, MPLS VPNs, traffic engineering; still essential to carrier infrastructure as of 2026.

Cisco's next-generation router designed for ISPs and internet backbone; first deployed 1997; multigigabit crossbar fabric sustaining 80 Gbps; interfaces OC-3/OC-12/OC-48, Gigabit Ethernet, Packet over SONET, ATM; Aberdeen described as 'appropriate and necessary'; sold 1,000 units by November 1998; became a major commercial success; still referenced in carrier networks.

High-end ATM broadband switch from StrataCom acquisition; first integration point for Tag Switching and Cisco IOS; BXM Broadband Switching Modules based on Stratm Technology; key to Cisco's ISP market positioning; eventually superseded by IP/MPLS platforms.

High-performance ASIC set for ATM switching and traffic management from StrataCom; features complete ABR support, virtual path trunking, weighted fair queuing, multicast; enables higher-density lower-cost ATM switching; integrated into BPX platforms.

Aberdeen-era workhorse enterprise and service provider router; target for NetFlow and Tag Switching capabilities; VIP2 (Versatile Interface Processor 2) enhanced with high-speed interfaces; 4x capacity increase through new processors; eventually superseded by 7600, ASR 1000, and 12000 GSR series.

Enterprise internet commerce platform; components: transaction engine (Financial eXchange), application portfolio (Catalog, Digital Delivery, Chat, Note), Oasis distributed architecture, Workplace management tools; EDI integration driver; CORBA and DCOM neutral via abstraction layer; clients included Broderbund, MicroWarehouse, Multiple Zones, Comdisco, Scholastic.

tech_name: Oasis (Object Architecture and Scalable Interoperating System)
notes: InterWorld's distributed transaction processing platform; abstraction layer between applications and object architectures (CORBA/DCOM); avoids technology lock-in; remote object marshaling to minimize network communication; eliminates universal messaging queue bottlenecks; dissolved with InterWorld.

OMG standard for distributed object communication; Aberdeen reports Oracle and BroadVision promoting CORBA for enterprise internet commerce; InterWorld's Oasis neutral between CORBA and DCOM; CORBA largely superseded by REST, SOAP, and API-based integration by 2010.

Microsoft's distributed object model for Windows-based internet commerce; competing with CORBA for enterprise internet commerce standard; InterWorld's Oasis supports DCOM without translation gateways; DCOM largely obsolete, superseded by WCF, REST, and cloud APIs.

Electronic document interchange for B2B transactions (purchase orders, invoices); Fortune 1000 standard for supply chain integration; Commerce eXchange supports EDI via driver for B2B channel; still used in legacy supply chain systems but being replaced by API-based integration and EDI-over-AS2/AS4.

Aberdeen notes Commerce eXchange uses SSL to protect sessions between browser and server; RC4 and MD5 encryption; SSL deprecated in favor of TLS (Transport Layer Security); TLS 1.3 is current standard; SSL itself obsolete.

InterWorld's digital goods download component; compliant with Microsoft ESD guidelines and SPA EULA; integrates with software clearinghouses; precursor concept to modern app stores (App Store 2008, Steam, etc.).

The emerging category of software that Commerce eXchange exemplified; Aberdeen's analysis of this category anticipates modern e-commerce platforms (Shopify, Magento, Salesforce Commerce Cloud, etc.); the category grew to a multi-billion dollar market.

Named alongside Unix and mainframe as legitimately retaining the enterprise high-end; still sold as IBM i today.

Central subject of migration study. Aberdeen predicts end-of-support within 18 months of 1997. Long since discontinued.
Successor to Sybase System 10. Lacked row-level locking at time of study. Evolved into Adaptive Server Enterprise (ASE). Retired after SAP acquisition.

Target language for migration from Sybase Transact-SQL stored procedures via Informix's stored procedure parser.

Central to Nortel's multimedia vision. ATM was eventually displaced by Ethernet/MPLS/IP for enterprise networking. Nortel's ATM focus was a strategic risk.

X.25 and TDM packet switches. Popular in traditional WAN deployments.

Integration of telephony with computing. Nortel highlighted CTI as a differentiator. CTI became ubiquitous in call centers.
Fundamental enterprise networking concept discussed throughout study.
Referenced as providing survivability for six-sigma reliability in Nortel ATM networks.

Deployed as sole OS for LAN server, application server, database server, and communications server at the Company. NT 4.0 was current version. Significant scalability and stability problems documented.
Deployed for developer and call center operator desktops. Most stable workstations required 2-3 reboots per day at time of study.
Replaced by NT mandate. OS/2 Warp 3 for call center servers; OS/2 Warp 4 for development; OS/2 Connect for workstations. Described as very stable before migration.

Deployed in call center operations. Documented: database crashes requiring hours/days to rebuild, frequent corruption, poor cache management, inadequate referential integrity and replication. Lacked row-level locking.
Deployed for mobile users under the 1995 Microsoft mandate.

ODMG-compliant object database. Acquired by Informix 1998; absorbed into IBM portfolio 2001. Technology lineage ended.

Pure object databases remained niche market. By 1999 IDC reported $211M OODBMS revenue vs $11.1B relational/object-relational. Still used in specialized domains.
Remained dominant. IDC 1999: $11.1B RDBMS revenue. Annual growth rate 18.2% through 2004 vs 12.5% for OODBMS.
ODMG-standard SQL-like query language for object databases. Superseded by SQL3/SQL:1999 object-relational extensions and JDO.

Synchronized demand management solution with object architecture. Aberdeen praised its design as capable of leading next technology phase. Market fate unknown; demand management consolidated into SAP Oracle and i2 Technologies.

Object-oriented programming as applied to enterprise applications. Became dominant paradigm through Java and C++ in enterprise software. Aberdeen identifies as key to adaptable core business processes.

Aberdeen's concept of electronically-enabled virtual enterprises via object technology. Realized through web services SOA microservices and B2B integration platforms.

Single-sideband FM technique for two-way broadcast-quality video over twisted pair phone wire. Product configuration: PBX switch (5-100 users) + modem-sized desktop device. Below $3000/user. Appears obsolete; company did not survive.
Existing phone line used as VidModem transport. VidModem key value proposition: no rewiring required. Same infrastructure later used for DSL broadband.
VidModem interfaced with ATM as wide area transport. Telcos promoting ATM backed VidModem. ATM itself declined as IP/MPLS and Ethernet WAN superseded it.
Aberdeen's reference case for telco deployment risk: ISDN applications died waiting for telco buildout. VidModem faced same risk. ISDN largely obsolete by 2010.
IP-based video conferencing standard. Aberdeen does not mention H.323 (ratified 1996) — competitor to VidModem approach. H.323 ultimately displaced analog FM video.
Aberdeen coins 'CTVI' (Computer Telephony Video Integration) for VidModem's seamless computer-phone-video integration. CTI became standard call center technology without VidModem.
Proprietary analog technique enabling NTSC PAL S-VHS broadcast quality video over single twisted pair alongside voice. Novel analog approach in an increasingly digital world.
IP-based browser-native video conferencing that ultimately solved the problem VidModem attempted. Zoom Teams Meet built on WebRTC/SIP foundations.
VidModem switch installed next to PBX. No rewiring required. PBX itself gradually displaced by VoIP/UCaaS platforms.

Oracle InterOffice was discontinued in the late 1990s as Oracle shifted strategy toward Oracle Collaboration Suite and later Oracle WebCenter. Never achieved significant market penetration against Exchange and Notes. | Successor: Oracle Collaboration Suite / Oracle WebCenter
Earlier monolithic Oracle messaging/office product that Oracle InterOffice was designed to supersede. Discontinued. | Successor: Oracle InterOffice (1995), Oracle Collaboration Suite (2003)
Standalone Oracle document management product deconstructed into Oracle InterOffice's modular DM component. Discontinued as a standalone product. | Successor: Oracle InterOffice DM module
Oracle Workflow product evolved; Oracle still provides workflow capabilities in Oracle E-Business Suite and Oracle Cloud applications. | Successor: Oracle BPEL Process Manager / Oracle Cloud Workflow
Oracle's full-text search/retrieval technology evolved into Oracle Text (formerly Oracle interMedia Text), still available in Oracle Database as of 2026. | Successor: Oracle Text (Oracle Database)

Oracle Database remains actively developed and deployed as of 2026 (Oracle 23c). Oracle System 7 was the version contemporaneous with this study; Oracle InterOffice used Oracle DB as its data store. | Successor: Oracle Database 23c

PL/SQL is Oracle's procedural extension to SQL, still actively used in Oracle Database development as of 2026. The study cites PL/SQL as a mechanism for direct server-side data access in InterOffice. | Successor: PL/SQL (Oracle Database 23c)
Oracle PowerObjects RAD environment was discontinued circa 1998; replaced by Oracle Developer/Forms and JDeveloper. | Successor: Oracle JDeveloper / Oracle APEX

OLE Automation and OCX evolved into ActiveX (renamed 1996), then into COM/DCOM and .NET COM Interop. OCX concept continues in modern Windows development as COM components. | Successor: ActiveX, COM, .NET

MAPI 1.0 was the standard Windows messaging API. MAPI remains present in modern Windows and Outlook as Extended MAPI, used primarily by legacy enterprise email integrations as of 2026. | Successor: Extended MAPI (Outlook)

The MAPI SPI allowed back-end messaging stores to expose capabilities to MAPI clients. Remains architecturally present in modern Outlook/Exchange integrations as of 2026. | Successor: Extended MAPI / Outlook Transport Provider

ODMA was a standard API for integrating desktop applications with document management systems. Largely abandoned by the mid-2000s as DM systems moved to web-based interfaces and SharePoint emerged. | Successor: Microsoft SharePoint / OpenText

WfMC's Workflow API was an industry standard attempt at workflow interoperability. Superseded by BPEL4WS, BPMN 2.0, and modern workflow automation platforms. | Successor: BPMN 2.0, BPEL, Camunda, Airflow

Java released publicly in 1995; remains one of the most widely used programming languages and platforms globally as of 2026 (Java SE 21 LTS current). The study anticipates Oracle integrating InterOffice with Java for distributed object access. | Successor: Java SE 21 / Jakarta EE
Web browsers became the universal client interface; browser technology evolved dramatically and is now the dominant computing platform as of 2026. | Successor: Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge

SMTP remains the foundational Internet email transport protocol as of 2026. The study references SMTP gateways as an early but inadequate Internet integration approach. | Successor: SMTP (RFC 5321, 2008)

Internet firewalls were nascent in 1995; technology evolved into stateful inspection, next-generation firewalls (NGFW), and cloud-based security services as of 2026. | Successor: NGFW, Cloud Security (Palo Alto, Zscaler)

Windows NT 3.51 mentioned as a supported server and client platform for Oracle InterOffice. NT kernel architecture evolved into Windows 2000, XP, and all modern Windows versions; NT 3.51 itself obsolete. | Successor: Windows Server 2025
Oracle InterOffice client supported Windows 95; Windows 95 reached end of mainstream support in 2001 and extended support in 2002. | Successor: Windows 11
Oracle InterOffice client supported Windows 3.1; end of mainstream support December 2001. | Successor: Windows 11
Motif X Window GUI client supported by Oracle InterOffice for Unix workstation users; largely obsolete, superseded by modern GUI toolkits. | Successor: GTK, Qt
Oracle InterOffice Macintosh client supported; Classic Mac OS superseded by Mac OS X (now macOS) in 2001; macOS Sequoia current as of 2026. | Successor: macOS Sequoia
Oracle InterOffice supported ASCII terminal access as a minimal-capability client. ASCII terminal use in mainstream IT obsolete; replaced by web and GUI clients. | Successor: Web browsers
Oracle was working with manufacturers on PDA clients for InterOffice; PDA devices evolved into smartphones and tablets as of 2026. | Successor: iOS, Android smartphones and tablets

HP-9000 PA-RISC server family cited as Oracle InterOffice supported platform. HP-9000/PA-RISC servers reached end of life; HP transitioned enterprise servers to Itanium (Integrity) and then x86. | Successor: HPE ProLiant (x86)

Sun SPARC cited as Oracle InterOffice supported platform. Oracle acquired Sun (2010); Oracle continues SPARC/Solaris development but at reduced investment; Oracle SPARC servers still sold in niche contexts. | Successor: Oracle SPARC M8 / Oracle Solaris 11

IBM RS/6000 with AIX cited as Oracle InterOffice supported platform. RS/6000 evolved into IBM Power Systems; AIX remains actively supported for IBM Power as of 2026. | Successor: IBM Power10 / AIX 7.3

Microsoft Exchange cited as a groupware competitor. Exchange Server remains actively developed and widely deployed as Exchange Server 2019 and Exchange Online (Microsoft 365) as of 2026. | Successor: Microsoft Exchange Online / Microsoft 365

Lotus Notes cited as primary groupware competitor. Acquired by IBM (1995); evolved through IBM Lotus Notes to HCL Notes (2019). HCL Notes still actively used in enterprises as of 2026. | Successor: HCL Notes / HCL Domino

SQL remains the standard language for relational databases as of 2026 (SQL:2023 current). The study highlights SQL access to Oracle InterOffice data as a key architectural differentiator. | Successor: SQL:2023

End-to-end encryption referenced as a method for securing high-value Internet transactions; the concept is now a foundational security requirement across all communications platforms as of 2026. | Successor: TLS 1.3, Signal Protocol, PGP
Still in active use as OpenEdge ABL; large installed base but considered legacy for new projects.
Progress v8.0 object component system; superseded by OERA reference architecture and later web frameworks.
VBX replaced by OCX/ActiveX in 1996; completely obsolete today.
Still widely supported but largely superseded by JDBC, ORM frameworks, and native drivers.
Category largely displaced by web application frameworks, IDEs, and cloud-native tooling.
RAD concepts evolved into Agile methodologies; still widely practiced under different names.
SPARC-based SMP system with 4–64 CPUs; up to 5TB online storage; 1.76 GB/s internal bandwidth. Sold to Sun in 1996; evolved into Sun Enterprise 10000 (Starfire).
Sun Solaris UNIX 5.4 enhanced with processor partitioning, memory management, and hot-swap RAS features for enterprise workloads.
RISC processor architecture; Cray CS6400 was binary-compatible with Sun SPARC, enabling access to 10000+ ISV applications.
SMP scaling to 64 CPUs was the key architecture for high-end RDBMS workloads; succeeded by NUMA and cloud scale-out architectures.
MPP system using DEC Alpha chipset; for scientific workloads >5TB. Later evolved into T3E under SGI ownership.
Databases at 20GB–5TB scale requiring specialized hardware. The VLDB concept is now mainstream via cloud data warehouses.
mature, aberdeen-1995-rdbms-cray-research-inc, Relational database engines optimized for SMP/MPP parallelism to support OLTP and DSS at scale.
SPARC64 still produced by Fujitsu through ~2020; Oracle phased out SPARC chip development in 2017.
Oracle Solaris still maintained but declining; last major release Solaris 11.4 in 2018.
SMP became standard in server computing; foundational to modern multi-core architectures.
SPARCcluster HA. HA clustering became standard enterprise practice; evolved to include cloud HA.
TPC-C benchmarks referenced for commercial DB performance comparison.
Commercial Unix largely displaced by Linux for new workloads; legacy deployments still active.
Oracle/DB2/Sybase on Unix servers was primary commercial use case; largely migrated to Linux x86 by 2010.
SCRUM formalized with Schwaber/Sutherland; Agile Manifesto (2001) endorsed sprint-based approach; now dominant software dev methodology worldwide.
ADM flagship product with 4 modules including Product Management Facility for SCRUM; obsolete as standalone tool.
Planning-heavy waterfall methods criticized in study; still used in regulated industries but displaced by Agile for most software.
Aberdeen notes most IS organizations not yet using OOP for mission-critical apps in 1995; OOP became universal by 2000s.
1-6 week sprint with cross-functional team delivering executable code; now standard Agile practice globally.
Windows 95 used as ISV beta-program example; shipped August 24, 1995; 400,000 beta users. EOL December 31, 2001.
Two-tier application model dominant in early 1990s; Aberdeen argues this should be evolved rather than abandoned.
Described as '1990s client for the masses'; server-centric execution model.
Aberdeen notes Java is limited by language/class library constraints for complex OLTP in 1996; prediction was accurate for the era.
Aberdeen notes suppliers moving away from CGI toward web-server-to-application-server partitioning; confirmed by Apache/IIS app servers.
Cited as best suited for client/server not browser model; confirmed — complex OLAP stayed thick-client until modern SaaS BI.
